sections.section_title: Your facility is doing well in this area.
Your facility could improve in this area.
This doesn't apply to your facility.
7. Interoperable Communications with Emergency Medical Response

question_items.question_text: 7. Interoperable Communications with Emergency Medical Response

items.label: Regular employee training on suspicious activity recognition
Visible signage promoting the campaign
Clear reporting procedures for suspicious activity

Consult your local PSA for information about the 'If You See Something, Say Something' public awareness campaign. For more information, visit the DHS website at https://www.dhs.gov/see-something-say-something/campaign-materials.
Annual or regular onsite visits
Facility tour provided to law enforcement
Emergency response booklet shared
Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
Develop and provide emergency responders with an offsite emergency responder booklet that includes facility floor plans and identifies utility shutoffs, outside exits, etc.
Direct radio communication capability
Emergency communication protocols
Regular communication testing
Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.

Explore the option of an interconnect system, such as a gateway that can allow communication between radio systems that are otherwise incompatible because they operate on different frequency bands or using different technologies.
Annual or regular onsite visits
Facility tour provided to fire responders
Fire safety information shared
Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
Direct radio communication capability
Emergency communication protocols
Regular communication testing
Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.
Annual or regular onsite visits
Facility tour provided to medical responders
Medical emergency information shared
Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
Direct radio communication capability
Emergency communication protocols
Regular communication testing
Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.

Explore the option of an interconnect system, such as a gateway that can allow communication between radio systems that are otherwise incompatible because they operate on different frequency bands or using different technologies.
Joint Terrorism Task Force (JTTF) participation
Regular information sharing protocols
Designated FBI liaison contact

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the JTTF. Information about JTTFs is also available via the FBI website, at https://www.fbi.gov/investigate/terrorism/joint-terrorism-task-forces.
Active ISAC membership
Regular threat intelligence updates
Information sharing protocols established

Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate ISAC. Additional information is available via the National Council of ISACs website, at https://www.nationalisacs.org.
HSIN community membership
Regular information sharing activities
Threat intelligence access
Consult your local PSA to become a member of the HSIN community. Information about HSIN is also available via the DHS website, at https://www.dhs.gov/homeland-security-information-network-hsin.
Active InfraGard membership
Local chapter participation
Information sharing activities
Consult your local PSA about becoming a member of InfraGard. Additional information is also available via the InfraGard website, at https://www.infragard.org.
DHS information sharing
DOE information sharing
Other federal agency contacts
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with federal agencies.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with federal agencies.
State fusion center participation
Local law enforcement information sharing
Emergency management coordination
Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with state and local agencies.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with state and local agencies.
Establish a liaison with state and/or major urban area fusion centers to enhance communication and information sharing.
Regular meetings with neighboring facilities
Information sharing protocols established
Emergency coordination procedures
Establish relationships and open dialogue including regular meetings with neighboring facilities to enhance information sharing and coordination.
Dedicated security manager position
Clear reporting structure to senior management
Security manager with appropriate qualifications
Consider hiring a security manager or designate an employee to act as a security manager and develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility.
Written comprehensive security plan
Plan addresses physical security systems
Plan includes emergency response procedures
Plan addresses employee security awareness

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address issues such as perimeter security, access control, electronic security systems (including video surveillance), physical security inspection programs, and employee security awareness training programs.

Create a detailed security plan for your facility. The plan should cover things like identifying potential problems, looking at weak spots, managing risks, what to do in emergencies, and how to keep your business running during problems.
Consult
Regular security plan training for all personnel
Training covers emergency response procedures
Training includes security awareness topics
Training documentation and records maintained
Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address issues such as perimeter security, access control, electronic security systems (including video surveillance), physical security inspection programs, and employee security awareness training programs.

Create a detailed security plan for your facility. The plan should cover things like identifying potential problems, looking at weak spots, managing risks, what to do in emergencies, and how to keep your business running during problems.
Consult
Regular coordination meetings with law enforcement
Mutual-aid agreements established
Facility information shared with law enforcement
Joint training and exercises conducted
Coordinate the security plan with local law enforcement. Consult
Coordinate the security plan with local law enforcement, and establish mutual-aid agreements to enhance emergency response capabilities.
Establish a liaison and regular communication with local law enforcement and other first responders to enhance coordination and information sharing.
Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, the location of critical assets, utility shutoffs, etc.) with local law enforcement to enhance their response capabilities.
Involve facility operators, security personnel, local police, and other government services, as appropriate, in the development and review of the security plan.
Conduct training and exercises with law enforcement and emergency responders to familiarize them with the facility and its security procedures.
Initial security training for new employees
Annual security awareness training
Suspicious activity reporting training
Emergency response procedures training

Train employees to do the following: identify their responsibilities under the facility's security plan, understand the facility's security policies and procedures, and recognize and report suspicious activities.

Train employees on why the facility requires protection strategies, what actions are required to protect specific assets and areas, what employees' security responsibilities are and how they can be fulfilled, how employees can report program violations, and how employees can identify indicators of risk or danger and how to respond appropriately.
Train employees on suspicious activity reporting procedures, security incident (e.g. bomb threat, active shooter) response procedures, and emergency evacuation procedures.
Enhance the level of employee security awareness through the distribution of a security-related newsletter, regular security briefings, or other awareness activities.
Regular security drills and exercises
Annual comprehensive exercise
Employee participation in exercises
First responder involvement in exercises
Test the security plan regularly with drills and tabletop exercises to ensure that adequate security measures are in place and that personnel are familiar with their roles and responsibilities.
Exercise the plan at least once annually to ensure it remains applicable as changes occur at the facility.
Exercise the plan regularly with employees and other appropriate personnel (e.g., building management, security personnel) to ensure familiarity with procedures.
Involve first responders in exercises to familiarize them with the facility and its security procedures.
Consult the Ready.gov website for more information, at https://www.ready.gov/exercises.
Regular security risk assessments
Looking at potential problems and weak spots
Identifying your most important assets
Ways to reduce risks
Conduct an assessment of possible security risks, including a review of threats to, and vulnerabilities of, facility operations and activities.
Identify key personnel responsible for security (e.g., the security manager or a designated representative) and first responders.

Identify critical assets or areas, including management and use of physical security systems, perimeter security, parking/delivery/standoff distance, locks and technologies, video surveillance systems, intrusion detection or alarm systems, illumination, key control program, and physical security inspection program.
Trained security personnel with appropriate certifications
24/7 security coverage
Appropriate equipment and communications
Consider implementing dedicated security personnel or contract security services to provide consistent security coverage.
Ensure security personnel receive appropriate training and certifications for their assigned duties.
Perimeter fencing with appropriate height and construction
Vehicle barriers and bollards at access points
Controlled access points with proper signage
Implement perimeter fencing with appropriate height and construction to establish clear facility boundaries.
Install vehicle barriers and bollards at critical access points to prevent unauthorized vehicle access.
Electronic access control at main entrances
Card-based or biometric access systems
Visitor management system
Implement electronic access control systems to manage and monitor facility access.
Establish a visitor management system to track and control visitor access.
Perimeter lighting with appropriate illumination
Parking area lighting
Backup power for critical lighting
Install security lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas to enhance visibility and deter unauthorized access.
Ensure lighting uniformity to eliminate dark areas and shadows where intruders could go undetected.
Adequate standoff distance between parking and buildings
Vehicle barriers to prevent close approach
Controlled parking areas with access restrictions
Establish adequate standoff distance between parking areas and buildings to prevent vehicle-borne threats.
Install vehicle barriers and bollards to prevent unauthorized vehicles from approaching buildings.
Secured doors and windows with appropriate locks
Glazing protection on ground floor windows
Controlled entry points with proper access controls
Implement appropriate locks and security measures on all doors and windows.
Install glazing protection on ground floor windows to prevent unauthorized entry.
Formal key issuance and tracking procedures
Key return procedures for departing employees
Regular key inventory and audit procedures
Implement a formal key control program to track and manage facility keys.
Establish procedures for key issuance, tracking, and return to prevent unauthorized access.
Motion Detection – automated alerts for movement in designated areas
Intrusion Detection – monitoring restricted areas for unauthorized entry
Object Left Behind Detection – alerting when items are placed and left unattended
People Counting – tracking the number of individuals in defined zones
Line Crossing Detection – alerts when people cross defined virtual boundaries
Loitering Detection – identifying individuals lingering in sensitive areas
Tailgating Detection – alerting when multiple people enter on single authorization
License Plate Recognition (LPR) – automated reading of vehicle license plates
Vehicle Analytics – vehicle counting and parking monitoring
Fire & Smoke Detection – early warning through video-based recognition
Heat Mapping – visualizing movement patterns for facility optimization
Facial Recognition – identifying individuals against watchlists (if legally compliant)
Consider upgrading to a digital/IP-based system to enable advanced video analytics capabilities
Evaluate the cost-benefit of implementing video analytics for your specific security requirements
Consult with security system integrators about available analytics solutions for your facility
Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive video surveillance system onsite. Site-specific factors must be considered when selecting components that comprise a particular system.
Consult
Perimeter surveillance coverage
Entry and exit point monitoring
Parking area surveillance
Critical asset monitoring
Evaluate video surveillance coverage of critical areas to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
Install cameras in areas such as the main entrance, at all vehicle access control points, guard booths, cashier booths, and elevator lobbies on every floor.
Dedicated monitoring staff
24/7 monitoring coverage
Response procedures for suspicious activity
Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the video surveillance system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of monitoring and observation.
Adequate recording duration and quality
Secure storage and backup systems
Video retention policies and procedures
Evaluate the need to record video surveillance footage, and select video recording and storage systems for the facility as appropriate.
Perimeter intrusion detection sensors
Door and window alarm systems
Motion detection systems
Central monitoring station
Consider implementing intrusion detection systems to provide early warning of unauthorized access attempts.
Install door and window alarm systems to detect unauthorized entry.
24/7 central station monitoring
On-site security personnel monitoring
Automated notification procedures
Implement central station monitoring or on-site security personnel monitoring for alarm systems.
Establish automated notification procedures for alarm activations.
Integrated access control and video surveillance
Coordinated alarm and response procedures
Centralized security management system
Consider integrating security systems to provide coordinated response capabilities and improve overall security effectiveness.
Implement centralized security management to coordinate system responses and improve incident management.
